items.q: Why was the Weimar Republic politically unstable?
items.correct: Proportional representation produced fragile coalitions; Article 48 enabled rule by decree; the 'stab-in-the-back' myth delegitimised the republic; extremists attacked from both left and right.
items.misconception: Weimar was unstable because Germans had never experienced democracy before.
items.random_wrong: Weimar was unstable because the Kaiser was still secretly in charge.
items.why: Germany had decades of parliamentary politics under the Kaiserreich. Weimar's problems were structural and contingent, not cultural incapacity for democracy.

items.q: Why was the Berlin Wall built in 1961?
items.correct: To stop the mass flow of East Germans to the West via Berlin (millions had fled), which was draining the GDR of skilled workers and threatening the regime's survival.
items.misconception: The Berlin Wall was built to stop a Western invasion of East Berlin.
items.random_wrong: The Berlin Wall was built by NATO to keep East Germans out of West Germany.
items.why: The Wall faced inward to trap GDR citizens, not outward against invasion. Students miss that it was an admission of weakness — people were voting with their feet.

items.q: Why did the Treaty of Versailles cause resentment in Germany?
items.correct: It assigned sole war guilt to Germany (Article 231) and imposed harsh reparations, territorial losses and military restrictions.
items.misconception: Because Germany lost more soldiers than any other country in the war.
items.random_wrong: Because Germany was not invited to sign it.
items.why: Students conflate the harshness of the treaty with battlefield losses. Germany WAS excluded from negotiations, but the resentment was about the terms (especially the war-guilt clause) — not exclusion from signing.

items.q: Who was murdered with Liebknecht in January 1919?
items.why: Correct answer: Luxemburg. “Eisner” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did the Wannsee Conference coordinate?
items.why: It's Final Solution. “Operation Barbarossa” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the Dawes Plan agreed?
items.why: The real figure is 1924 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
